context: Defines stable-surface contracts for redaction, profiles, freshness, command/event/audit logs, correction, gaps, authority, dependencies, migration, risk, exposure, lifecycle, observability, topology, provenance, schema compatibility, adapters, and capability matching.
assumptions: Stable and adapter-facing surfaces need explicit consumption contracts before external reliance
Root topology should become schema-backed before module linkage and runtime reality checks
Adapter capability posture should be explicit before remote integrations are described as stable
Write-capable surfaces need audit linkage before command semantics are exposed as stable
Append-only event surfaces need correction semantics before consumers treat event history as stable truth
Partial implementation gaps need explicit owner, blocking posture, remediation, and consumer guidance before being treated as non-blocking
Overlapping stable surfaces need declared canonical, derived, alias, conflict, and migration posture before consumers merge claims

Stable surfaces that depend on other surfaces need explicit hard, optional, blocking, derivation, emission, and consumption ordering before validation or routing treats them as independently consumable
Deprecated or removed stable surfaces need replacement, migration, affected version, removal, and validation behavior before consumers can safely move
Agent-consumable surfaces need canonical risk categories before routing, adapter, command, or release policy treats risk labels as comparable
Local-only and remote-capable surfaces need exposure constraints before local exports are reused as adapter or API contracts
Commands and adapters need audit-log requirements before consumers rely on action traces, receipts, policy decisions, or failure reconstruction
Long-running operational objects need explicit lifecycle state-machine profiles before implementations compare status, retries, cancellation, timeout, cleanup, or terminal behavior
Validation and routing decisions need observable rule, location, dependency, route, skip, and next-action metadata before agents can debug why a surface loaded, failed, or was skipped

sections.content: Sensitive-surface governance is metadata-first. AODS does not infer private data by scanning text, and it does not perform runtime redaction. A surface declares whether sensitive payloads can appear, which sensitive classes apply, and which redaction posture governs broader exposure. sensitive_classes is limited to pii, credentials, medical, financial, proprietary, regulated, secret, or other. redaction_posture is none, omit, redact, handle-only, or debug-only. none means no sensitive payload is expected. omit means the sensitive field or section must not appear in the exposed surface. redact means a value may appear only after irreversible masking. handle-only means a stable surface may carry an opaque handle but not the underlying secret or personal value. debug-only means the data may exist only in local diagnostic or evidence surfaces and must not become stable public output. When has_sensitive_payloads is true, sensitive_classes and exposure_policy are mandatory because a boolean alone cannot tell an agent how to avoid leakage. This model is intentionally not a privacy framework. It is a trust-boundary marker that keeps stable consumption from treating sensitive examples, debug traces, credentials, customer identifiers, or regulated values as ordinary reusable documentation. The negative validation strategy is deterministic: a module that marks has_sensitive_payloads=true without sensitive_classes or exposure_policy fails validation, and manifest/module redaction summaries must mirror when either side declares them.

A contract completeness profile declares the minimum information required before a surface can be consumed as stable. v0.7 defines only three profiles. read-model covers read-only data surfaces such as generated indexes, schema views, summaries, or inventory outputs. It must identify the data source, field set, freshness posture, identity or key semantics, and failure or absence semantics. Stable read-model contracts must declare read_model.freshness with snapshot_id, exported_at, source_watermark, and staleness so consumers can tell which source snapshot they are reading. staleness may be current, stale, partial, or unknown. partial snapshots must explain partial_semantics or partial_reason before stable consumption. command covers action surfaces such as CLI commands, hooks, workflows, or agent-triggered operations. It must identify input shape, side effects, preconditions, authorization or actor boundary, idempotency posture, observable outputs, and failure semantics. Stable write-capable command surfaces should also link command, receipt, and event or projection semantics before external consumers rely on writes. implementation-linkage covers the design-to-reality bridge used by the topology pilot. It must identify implementation repository, path or path pattern, implementation status, optional PR or issue references, and the authority surface that owns the claim. Profiles do not define every possible field in the ecosystem. They define the lowest safe floor for stable consumption, so a consumer can tell whether it is reading data, invoking action, or following a design-to-implementation link. A stable surface without a profile remains under-specified even when prose is clear. The validator boundary for this round is deterministic and metadata based: read-model profile must declare freshness and watermark metadata; manifest/module contract summaries still mirror the profile only.

A write-capable stable surface should describe its command, receipt, and event or projection linkage before agents or adapters depend on it. command means the requested write intent: actor boundary, source, target, payload schema, schema version, preconditions, idempotency key, side effects, and authorization posture. receipt means the durable attempt record: receipt_id, command_ref, status, accepted_at or rejected_at, rejection_category, observable_outputs, and audit_anchor. event or projection means the observable state consequence: event_ref or projection_ref, emitted_at or materialized_at, schema version, ordering key, replay posture, and consumer guidance. triad linkage means the command points to the receipt, the receipt points back to the command and forward to any event or projection, and the event or projection can be traced to the receipt for audit and replay. Missing pieces may be declared as not-applicable only with an authority reason and consumer guidance. This boundary does not execute commands, require an event bus runtime, define correction or supersession semantics, create a general workflow engine, or guarantee exactly-once delivery. It only prevents stable write-capable surfaces from relying on prose-only auditability.

An append-only event surface must not silently rewrite historical truth. Stable event consumers need explicit correction and supersession semantics before event history, timelines, or projections are treated as authoritative. correction_event means a new event that names correction_of, correction_actor, correction_source, correction_reason, corrected_fields, and replacement event posture without mutating the original event. supersession_link means an event may declare supersedes, superseded_by, replacement_event, and effective_at so consumers can identify which record currently carries the preferred truth. retraction means a correction class that states retraction_reason, retracted_event, replacement_event when one exists, and whether consumers should hide, mark, or preserve the original event. projection guidance means the authority surface tells readers how corrected events should appear in read models, timelines, exports, and audit views; it distinguishes historical, advisory, blocking, and replacement posture. This boundary does not allow history rewrite, implement an event store runtime, run automatic replay, migrate historical data, or decide domain truth by itself. Human review still owns whether the correction reason is adequate and whether a projection can safely consume the corrected event.

A partial implementation or known gap marker records an intentional gap without treating natural-language notes as enough for stable consumption. partial implementation means a surface declares that some contract fields, lifecycle states, validation checks, fixtures, evidence anchors, or runtime behaviors are not complete. known gap means a named omission with gap_id, surface_ref, missing_capabilities, blocking_status, owner, expected_remediation, target_posture, evidence_refs, and consumer guidance. missing_capabilities names the absent capability, field, fixture, evidence, lifecycle transition, validator gate, implementation path, or runtime behavior. blocking_status may be non-blocking, warning, strict-blocking, release-blocking, drift-blocking, security-blocking, or unknown. owner identifies the accountable project role or surface owner, not a vague team note. expected_remediation describes the smallest next action and review gate needed to remove or downgrade the gap. consumer guidance tells agents whether to use the surface, downgrade, require human review, or avoid stable consumption. This boundary does not create a roadmap system, automatic waiver, release override, backlog tracker, or validator bypass.

sections.topic: overlapping surface ownership and authority hierarchy
sections.content: Overlapping surfaces are stable only when authority hierarchy is explicit. overlapping surfaces means two or more modules, sections, artifacts, human surfaces, generated indexes, commands, events, or views describe the same object, lifecycle state, command, event, decision, or exported claim. canonical authority means the single surface whose claim wins for a named overlap_scope. derived surface means a surface generated from, summarized from, projected from, or constrained by the canonical authority; it must name source_authority, derivation_rule, freshness_posture, and divergence_policy before stable consumption. alias means another stable identifier, route, or surface name that intentionally points at the same authority; aliases must name alias_of, allowed_use, and deprecation posture when the alias is temporary. conflict policy means the declared response when surfaces disagree: canonical-wins, derived-refresh-required, human-review-required, block-consumption, or deprecated-alias. migration guidance means the replacement, compatibility window, consumer action, and removal posture for aliases, deprecated derived surfaces, or authority moves. This boundary does not create an automatic conflict resolver, infer ownership from paths or names, fetch external corpora, run cross_corpus authority runtime, or choose domain truth. It only makes overlap consumable without asking agents to guess which surface owns the claim.

dependency ordering declares how stable surfaces depend on one another before validation, routing, release review, or agent consumption treats them as independently usable. requires means a hard dependency: the source surface cannot be consumed as stable unless the target surface exists and has an acceptable lifecycle and contract posture. blocks means a surface prevents another surface from promotion, release, routing, or stable consumption until a named condition is resolved. derives_from means a generated, summarized, projected, indexed, or exported surface is derived from a source authority and must declare derivation_rule, freshness posture, and consumer guidance. emits means a command, workflow, adapter, event source, or runtime contract produces a receipt, event, projection, export, or generated surface. consumes means a command, adapter, validator, route, export, or generated artifact reads another surface as input. optional dependency means a dependency may improve fidelity, routing, evidence, or diagnostics but must name fallback behavior and degraded posture when absent. dependency_strength is hard, optional, advisory, or unknown; hard dependencies block stable consumption when missing or unstable, optional dependencies require fallback guidance, advisory dependencies provide context only, and unknown dependencies require human review before stable consumption. This boundary does not create a package manager, runtime scheduler, cross_repo dependency executor, automatic topological build runner, or remote fetch mechanism. It only gives consumers a deterministic vocabulary for dependency order and degraded use.

sections.topic: deprecation and migration format for stable surfaces
sections.content: deprecation marks a stable surface as still readable but no longer preferred. A deprecated surface must identify deprecated_surface, deprecated_at, deprecation_reason, lifecycle_state, owner, and consumer_action before agents treat it as stable enough to consume. replacement means a resolvable replacement_ref or replacement_surface with replacement_kind and authority posture; no-replacement is allowed only with an explicit reason and consumer guidance. migration guidance means the concrete consumer action needed to move from the deprecated surface to the replacement, including field_mapping, command_mapping, event_mapping, export_mapping, compatibility_notes, and manual_review_required when migration cannot be mechanical. affected_versions names affected_versions, first_deprecated_version, removal_version, compatibility_window, and removal_posture so consumers can tell which corpus, module, command, event, field, or export versions are impacted. validation behavior describes before-removal and after-removal gates: before removal, validators may warn, require replacement links, or require review; after removal, stable consumers should fail on unresolved uses unless an explicit compatibility window or known-gap posture still applies. This boundary does not create an automatic_migration_tool, rewrite consumers, provide a runtime_compatibility_shim, transform stored data, or guarantee backward compatibility. It only gives deprecated, replaced, and removed surfaces a stable metadata shape.

sections.topic: standard risk taxonomy for agent-consumable surfaces
sections.content: canonical risk categories let agent-consumable surfaces use comparable risk labels before routing, adapter, command, resource, or release policy treats a surface as safe. read risk describes what can be exposed when a surface is read, including sensitive payload, stale data, local-only information, or authority confusion. write risk describes state change caused by commands, adapters, resources, generated outputs, releases, or production mutation. credential risk marks token, secret, credential, handle-only, and debug-only exposure. filesystem risk marks local path, repo path, generated output, and stored artifact read or write access. network risk marks remote read, remote write, external fetch, public endpoint exposure, and egress. external-send risk marks email, issue, pull request, release, public post, or other external message surfaces. cost risk marks paid API, compute, storage, quota, and rate-limit consumption. production-mutation risk marks production data, config, deploy, release, and irreversible operational changes. human approval states when a risk label needs review, approval, strict gate, release gate, or security gate. capability negotiation may compare provider capability risk labels with consumer risk floors, but this metadata does not create runtime_policy_engine behavior. This boundary does not create a permission_broker, dynamic_risk_scanner, approval_workflow, sandbox, secret manager, network broker, or cost accounting runtime.

local-only, local-export, remote-read, remote-write, and adapter-facing are exposure classes for stable surfaces, not transport implementations. local-only means a surface may expose local paths, debug details, handles, environment assumptions, repo-only evidence, or other local diagnostic material for local agents only; it must declare consumer_scope, allowed_locations, risk_floor, redaction posture, freshness posture when data is read, compatibility posture when consumers cache it, and the reason it is not remote-safe. local-export means a file or artifact is generated locally and may be shared only across its declared export boundary; it must declare export_format, artifact scope, redaction, freshness, compatibility, and consumer guidance so a generated file is not mistaken for an API contract. remote-read means an external consumer may read the surface through a remote, API, hosted, or adapter boundary; it adds auth boundary, redaction floor, freshness requirement, compatibility policy, rate/cost/network risk labels, and audit anchor expectations before stable consumption. remote-write means a remote consumer may mutate state through a command, adapter, resource, or workflow; it adds command, receipt, event or projection linkage, idempotency posture, policy decision, human approval when risk requires it, rollback posture, and production-mutation risk before the write is treated as stable. adapter-facing means a surface is stable for adapters only when adapter capability minimum, standard risk taxonomy labels, redaction, auth, freshness, compatibility, and evidence anchors are declared. upgrade gates are required before any local-only or local-export surface is promoted to remote-read, remote-write, or adapter-facing use: the replacement authority or exposure review must declare exposure_class, upgrade_gate, risk labels, redaction, auth, freshness, compatibility, audit anchor, and consumer guidance. Future validation may warn when a local-only surface is referenced by remote consumers without an explicit upgrade gate. This boundary does not create a remote_api_gateway, auth_runtime, network_broker, automatic_exposure_upgrader, sandbox, remote transport runtime, or policy enforcement runtime; human review still owns whether an exposure upgrade is safe.

sections.topic: audit-log requirements for commands and adapters
sections.content: audit-log requirements define the minimum metadata needed before commands or adapters can claim an auditable action trace. An auditable command or adapter surface must identify actor, source, target, command, idempotency key, policy decision, receipt reference, timestamp, and correlation identifiers. actor names the human, agent, service, adapter principal, or delegated role that requested the action. source names the stable surface, command profile, adapter capability, route, workflow entrypoint, or external-send surface that initiated the request. target names the object, resource, environment, repo path, endpoint, account, or production boundary affected by the action. command identifies command_ref, payload schema or schema version, side effect class, retry posture, and whether the action is read, write, remote-read, remote-write, or adapter-facing. idempotency key links retries and duplicate attempts so consumers can distinguish repeated requests from independent writes. policy decision records allow, deny, review-required, blocked, or not-applicable with decision source and risk labels. receipt reference links the audit record to command receipts and receipts/events by receipt_ref, event_or_projection_ref, status, failure category, and observable outputs. timestamp records requested_at plus accepted_at, rejected_at, completed_at, or emitted_at when those events exist. correlation identifiers include correlation_id, trace_id, run_id, task_id, or parent_command_ref so cross-surface traces remain joinable. Validation can warn when auditable command, remote-write, or adapter-facing surfaces lack this audit metadata. This boundary does not create an audit_log_store, workflow_engine, SIEM integration, observability_backend, policy engine, identity provider, or event bus runtime.

sections.topic: lifecycle state-machine profile for operational objects
sections.content: A lifecycle state-machine profile defines the minimum stable contract for long-running operational objects. operational objects are jobs, runs, sessions, exports, imports, deployments, migrations, tasks, workflows, or adapter operations whose state changes over time. lifecycle state is the authoritative machine state used for validation, routing, automation, and recovery; display status is a human-facing label and must not replace lifecycle state. initial states identify where an object may start after creation, import, resume, or restoration. terminal states identify states such as succeeded, failed, canceled, expired, superseded, or abandoned after which normal transitions stop unless an explicit correction or replacement event exists. transitions declare from_state, to_state, trigger, actor boundary, authority surface, and state transition authority. guards declare preconditions, policy gates, risk gates, approval gates, freshness checks, dependency checks, and known-gap handling before a transition is stable. timeout and expiration describe timeout_at, expires_at, staleness, timeout_action, expiration_action, and consumer guidance. retry policy describes retryable states, retry limits, backoff posture, idempotency requirements, and retry terminal behavior. cancellation semantics describe who can cancel, when cancellation is allowed, cancellation side effects, receipt linkage, and resulting terminal state. cleanup semantics describe cleanup trigger, resource scope, retained evidence, irreversible deletion posture, and consumer guidance. State changes must link to events or receipts through receipt_ref, event_or_projection_ref, audit anchor, or correction/supersession link when applicable. Future validation can warn when long-running operational objects lack lifecycle state-machine profile metadata. This boundary does not create a workflow_engine, scheduler, retry_runtime, cleanup_executor, object factory, state persistence runtime, or operational dashboard.

sections.topic: observability metadata for validation and routing decisions
sections.content: Validation and routing decisions need observability metadata before agents treat pass, fail, selected, skipped, or fallback outcomes as explainable. Validation observability must identify rule id, severity, source location, dependency path when relevant, and suggested next action. rule id names the stable validation rule or future validation boundary that produced the decision. severity names the gate level or diagnostic posture so consumers can tell whether the result blocks, warns, advises, or only informs. source location names the file, json pointer, section ref, artifact ref, or line and column when available. dependency path names the dependency chain, ref path, blocking edge, missing target, or route dependency that caused the decision. Routing observability must identify routing reason, selected modules, skipped modules, fallback posture, dependency path when relevant, and suggested next action. routing reason explains why a route selected, loaded, skipped, degraded, or fell back for a module or surface. selected modules name the modules included in the working set and the selection reason. skipped modules name modules excluded from the working set with skip reason and consumer guidance. suggested next action names the smallest remediation, review, command, or authoring action available without executing it. This boundary defines machine-readable explanation vocabulary only. It does not rewrite the CLI output subsystem, build a dashboard, create a trace store, create a graph database, change route ranking, implement telemetry storage, or make every diagnostic complete.

sections.topic: project topology and implementation alignment pilot
sections.content: The project_topology pilot exists to break design-only green loops. AODS validation can be fully green while a separate implementation repository is absent, stalled, or invisible. The pilot turns that external reality into a first-class root surface before it becomes a runtime gate. Root topology now lives at manifest.project_topology. It declares the design repository posture plus implementation_repos[]. Each repo entry carries id, locator, role, branch, status, and optional notes. design_repo uses the same minimum shape so the corpus can declare where authority lives before checking downstream implementation paths. Status is a claim about implementation reality, not proof by itself. Allowed status values for the pilot are not-started, planned, in-progress, partial, current, blocked, deprecated, and removed. Module implementation linkage now lives at module.meta.implementation and mirrors through manifest.modules[].implementation. It points from a module to repo_id, paths, status, optional pr_refs, authority_surface, evidence anchors, and acceptance criteria. pr_refs are trace anchors and do not replace path linkage. Paths are relative to the linked implementation repository. Evidence anchors live only in module.meta.implementation.evidence[]. Acceptance criteria live only in module.meta.implementation.acceptance_criteria[] and link contract requirements to evidence refs, validator rules, fixtures, or manual review posture. The manifest carries evidence_summary and acceptance_summary only. evidence_summary counts total, current, planned, stale, and blocked evidence. acceptance_summary counts total, satisfied, planned, partial, waived, blocked, and manual_review criteria. Current implementation linkage must declare at least one evidence anchor and one acceptance criterion so current status is reviewable and tied to a requirement. Evidence locators and criteria are declarative; validators may check repo-relative evidence locator existence under validate --reality when the linked repository root is resolvable, but validators do not execute evidence commands. validate --reality now emits a topology summary with linked_modules, unlinked_modules, checked_paths, missing_paths, evidence_total, checked_evidence_locators, missing_evidence_locators, and explicit unchecked_reason when repo locators cannot yet be resolved from the selected repo root. Non-goals remain cross-repo CI dispatch, automatic code ownership detection, fingerprint drift gates, arbitrary command execution, and closing issue 28 as duplicate. The staged path now reads: U-007 defined pilot semantics, U-008 landed root schema plus examples, U-009 landed module linkage schema and mirror rules, U-010 landed topology-aware reality summaries with explicit partial-check reporting, U-027 landed declarative implementation evidence, and U-029 landed implementation acceptance criteria.

Schema versioning defines how stable consumers survive change. AODS has three version layers: aods_v describes the framework contract, sys_v describes the corpus contract, and module.v describes one module contract. Stable and adapter-facing surfaces need a breaking_policy because consumers may cache, generate, or automate against them. strict means breaking changes are not accepted without a larger framework or corpus compatibility decision. versioned means breaking changes are allowed only with a declared compatibility window and migration guidance. experimental means breaking changes are allowed without a compatibility promise, and it is valid only for experimental, internal, or local-only surfaces. Breaking changes include removing a field, changing required or optional status at a stable boundary, changing type, removing enum values, changing field meaning, or changing side-effect semantics. Non-breaking changes include adding optional fields, clarifying prose, adding examples, adding non-authoritative evidence, or tightening validation wording without changing consumer behavior. Deprecated is a compatibility holding state; removed is terminal. A deprecated stable surface must name a replacement, compatibility window, or removal guidance before removed state. The minimal validation strategy is deterministic: manifest/module schema_versioning summaries must mirror when declared, and validation-rules records the future strict gate for breaking changes without building a migration engine in this round.

adapter-facing does not mean general negotiation. v0.7 only defines the minimum contract needed before a surface may claim stable adapter capability. An adapter-facing capability entry must identify adapter_id, capability_id, transport_scope, contract_profile, schema_version_policy, freshness_posture, redaction_posture, and at least one audit_anchor. transport_scope says whether the capability is local-only, hosted, or hybrid. contract_profile ties the adapter claim back to read-model, command, or a later explicit capability profile instead of inventing ad-hoc surface meanings. schema_version_policy tells consumers whether compatibility is strict, versioned, or experimental. freshness_posture tells whether the adapter claim is static, on-demand, or continuously refreshed. audit_anchor points at the authority or evidence surface that can justify the capability claim. Non-goals for this round are negotiation handshake formats, provider discovery, auth exchange, transport retries, fallback ranking, and dynamic capability probing. The purpose is narrower: prevent adapter-facing surfaces from becoming stable by vague prose alone.

Capability negotiation remains a deferred runtime protocol. The v0.8 re-triage defines only metadata boundaries so adapter-facing claims can be compared without creating a handshake. provider capability means a provider declares capability_id, contract_profile, schema_version_policy, transport_scope, freshness_posture, redaction_posture, limits, and evidence_anchor. consumer requirement means a consumer declares required capability_id, accepted contract_profile, required schema_version_policy, transport_scope needs, freshness requirement, redaction floor, and blocking posture. compatibility matching means deterministic metadata comparison of provider capability to consumer requirement; it can say compatible, incompatible, partial, or unknown. evidence link means provider and consumer claims can point at implementation evidence, acceptance criteria, fixture manifests, or authority sections. This re-triage does not introduce negotiation sessions, runtime discovery, auth exchange, provider selection, fallback ranking, dynamic probing, remote execution, or transport retries. If a future task needs those behaviors, it must start from these metadata fields and add a separate protocol surface instead of overloading adapter-facing prose.

Agent-consumable decisions are stable only when their source boundary is explicit. A decision artifact may declare decision_provenance to identify whether the decision is consumed by agents, held for human review, or internal. The basis states whether the decision comes directly from source evidence, from a derived summary, or from a mixed boundary. source_refs must point at sections or artifacts that authorize the decision. evidence_refs may point at proof anchors. summary_ref is required when the decision is derived from a summary or mixed with a summary because agents must know where synthesis happened. evidence_status is current, stale, unresolved, or withheld. consumption_gate is stable, review-required, blocked, or experimental. An agent-consumable decision cannot be stable when evidence is stale, unresolved, or withheld. Stale evidence means reviewers must refresh or downgrade before stable consumption. Unresolved evidence means agents must not treat the decision as authoritative. Withheld evidence means a stable consumer needs another allowed authority or an explicit review gate. This boundary does not create a full provenance framework, evidence warehouse, or cross-corpus resolver. It only prevents derived decision summaries from becoming stable agent instructions without source and evidence posture.

The stable-surface contract layer is deliberately small. It adds explicit metadata, profile names, read-model freshness and watermark metadata, command receipt event audit linkage, event correction and supersession vocabulary, partial implementation and known gap vocabulary, ownership and authority hierarchy vocabulary, dependency ordering vocabulary, deprecation and migration vocabulary, standard risk taxonomy vocabulary, local/remote exposure vocabulary, command/adapter audit-log requirements vocabulary, lifecycle state-machine vocabulary, validation/routing observability vocabulary, root topology semantics, compatibility language, decision provenance boundaries, a trimmed adapter capability floor, and metadata-only capability matching boundaries. It does not add automatic sensitive-data detection, redaction transforms, role-based access control, migration code generation, semantic schema diffing, multi-version negotiation, cross-repo fetch, evidence warehouse behavior, implementation fingerprinting, command execution, event bus runtime, event store replay, historical data migration, roadmap system behavior, automatic waiver, release override, automatic conflict resolver, ownership inference, cross-corpus authority runtime, package manager behavior, runtime scheduler, cross-repo dependency executor, automatic migration tool, consumer rewrite, runtime compatibility shim, runtime policy engine, permission broker, dynamic risk scanner, approval workflow, remote API gateway, auth runtime, network broker, automatic exposure upgrader, audit log store, workflow engine, scheduler, retry runtime, cleanup executor, CLI output subsystem rewrite, dashboard, trace store, graph database, SIEM integration, observability backend, runtime provider discovery, auth exchange, fallback ranking, or dynamic capability probing. It also does not make every future profile stable. full write-model runtime, resource ownership, export runtime, generated artifact runtime, remote transport runtime, audit storage runtime, state persistence runtime, telemetry storage, route ranking rewrite, and full adapter negotiation profiles remain deferred. The model favors deterministic sensors before broad inference. Required metadata can be checked by schema and validator. Human review still owns the meaning of a redaction policy, the sufficiency of a contract profile, whether a topology status is honest, whether a read-model source watermark is semantically sufficient, whether command receipt event linkage is operationally sufficient, whether an event correction reason is adequate, whether a known gap can be consumed without blocking, whether overlapping surface conflict policy is adequate, whether dependency ordering is sufficient for stable use, whether migration guidance is safe, whether a risk label is adequate, whether a local-only or local-export surface is safe to upgrade for remote-read, remote-write, or adapter-facing exposure, whether command or adapter audit metadata is sufficient to reconstruct action traces, whether a lifecycle state-machine profile is sufficient for long-running operational objects, whether validation or routing observability metadata is sufficient to explain a pass, fail, selection, skip, or fallback outcome, whether a decision summary is faithful to evidence, whether an adapter capability claim is adequately evidenced, and whether a partial capability match is acceptable. This boundary protects AODS from expanding into a general application governance framework before it has closed the trust gaps that block v0.7.

artifacts.usage: Canonical redaction posture values for stable-surface risk review.

rows: stable consumption allowed when other authority gates pass
sensitive payload must be excluded from exposed surface
stable output cannot carry the field or section
sensitive value may appear only after masking
stable output must not preserve original value
opaque reference may appear without underlying value
stable output may carry handles but not secrets
payload is local diagnostic or evidence material

artifacts.usage: Minimum field guide for v0.7 stable contract completeness profiles.

rows: source, fields, identity, freshness, absence_semantics, failure_semantics
input_shape, side_effects, preconditions, actor_boundary, idempotency, outputs, failure_semantics
action, workflow, CLI, hook, or agent operation

artifacts.usage: Minimum freshness and watermark fields for stable read-model contracts.

rows: authority or data source behind the read model
stable fields exposed by the read model
key or identity semantics for records or generated entries
stable identifier for the exported snapshot
timestamp or deterministic build time for the snapshot
source-side watermark, commit, offset, data version, or equivalent freshness anchor
current, stale, partial, or unknown currentness posture
how consumers should interpret partial or lagging snapshots
meaning of missing, empty, or withheld data
how generation or read failures are represented

artifacts.usage: Minimum fields for write-capable command receipt event triads.

rows: command_id, actor_boundary, source, target, payload_schema, schema_version, preconditions, idempotency_key, side_effects, authorization
receipt_id, command_ref, status, accepted_at or rejected_at, rejection_category, observable_outputs, audit_anchor
event_ref or projection_ref, receipt_ref, emitted_at or materialized_at, schema_version, ordering_key, replay_posture, consumer_guidance
command_ref, receipt_ref, event_ref or projection_ref, not_applicable_reason, authority_surface

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for write-capable command receipt event triads.

rows: executing writes requires runtime authorization, side-effect isolation, and rollback semantics beyond static contract metadata
durable delivery and projection materialization require infrastructure-specific semantics
correction, retraction, and supersession policy belongs to append-only event semantics
delivery guarantees depend on runtime transport, storage, and idempotency implementation

artifacts.usage: Minimum fields for append-only event correction and supersession boundaries.

rows: event_id, correction_of, correction_actor, correction_source, correction_reason, corrected_fields, replacement_event
new event records the correction without mutating original history
current preferred truth is traceable from prior event to replacement
invalid or withdrawn event remains auditable
consumers know whether to show historical, advisory, blocking, or replacement posture

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for event correction and supersession semantics.

rows: append-only semantics require preserving original events and adding correction records
not allowed for append-only stable event surfaces
storage, ordering, retention, and delivery behavior depends on implementation infrastructure
rebuilding read models requires runtime execution and migration policy beyond static metadata
whether a correction reason is sufficient remains owned by domain authority and human review

artifacts.usage: Minimum fields for partial implementation and known-gap metadata.

rows: names the gap and the surface that owns the declaration
states what stable consumers cannot rely on yet
tells agents whether to continue, warn, block, or require review
records the accountable owner and smallest remediation action
tells consumers how to use or avoid the partial surface

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for partial implementation and known-gap metadata.

rows: gap metadata records consumption posture but does not replace the project task ledger
known gaps must not silently bypass validation, release, or review gates
release eligibility remains a separate gate that may consume gap metadata but is not decided by it
declaring a gap explains partial posture but does not suppress deterministic validation failures

artifacts.usage: Minimum fields for overlapping surface ownership and authority hierarchy.

rows: identifies the single winning authority for a named overlap
keeps summaries, projections, indexes, and generated surfaces tied to their source
lets old names or routes point at the same authority without creating competing truth
tells consumers how to behave when overlapping claims disagree
supports authority moves, aliases, and deprecated derived surfaces

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for overlapping surface ownership and authority hierarchy.

rows: choosing truth across competing claims needs domain authority and review beyond static metadata
fetching, ranking, and merging authority across corpora requires a separate resolver and trust model
file paths, names, or git ownership are evidence hints, not declared authority
rewriting consumers or moving data requires runtime-specific migration machinery

artifacts.usage: Minimum fields for dependency ordering between stable surfaces.

rows: hard dependency that blocks stable use when missing or unstable
declares what prevents promotion, routing, release, or stable consumption
ties generated, projected, summarized, indexed, or exported surfaces to their source
links commands, workflows, adapters, or event sources to receipts, events, projections, exports, or generated surfaces
states what a command, adapter, validator, route, export, or generated artifact reads as input
documents non-blocking dependencies and degraded behavior when absent

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for dependency ordering between stable surfaces.

rows: dependency metadata describes stable consumption order but does not install, resolve, or version packages
execution order, retries, timeouts, and concurrency require runtime-specific semantics
running dependency checks across repositories requires repo resolution, trust, and command execution policy
building or regenerating derived surfaces is an execution workflow, not a static dependency declaration

artifacts.usage: Minimum fields for deprecation and migration metadata on stable surfaces.

rows: deprecated_surface, deprecated_at, deprecation_reason, lifecycle_state, owner, consumer_action
marks a stable surface as readable but no longer preferred
points consumers to the authority that replaces the deprecated surface
migration_steps, field_mapping, command_mapping, event_mapping, export_mapping, compatibility_notes, manual_review_required
describes how consumers move from old surface to replacement
tells consumers which versions remain readable and when removal becomes terminal
states when validators warn, require review, or fail unresolved use
consumer rewrite or runtime compatibility shim

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for deprecation and migration metadata.

rows: metadata describes migration guidance but does not transform files, commands, events, exports, or stored data
rewriting downstream agents, adapters, or generated surfaces requires consumer-specific execution semantics
runtime compatibility layers require execution, transport, and version negotiation beyond static metadata
compatibility depends on declared policy and human review rather than this metadata alone

artifacts.usage: Minimum risk categories for agent-consumable surfaces.

rows: risk_level, sensitive_payload, staleness, local_only_data, authority_confusion, consumer_floor
states what may be exposed when a surface is read
risk_level, mutation_scope, side_effects, idempotency_posture, rollback_posture, gate
states what can change when a command, adapter, or resource is invoked
marks token, secret, credential, and handle-only exposure posture
marks local path, repo path, generated output, and stored artifact risk
marks remote read, remote write, external fetch, public endpoint, and egress risk
marks external messages such as email, issue, pull request, release, or public post
marks paid API, compute, storage, quota, and rate-limit consumption
marks production data, config, deploy, release, and irreversible operational changes
states when a risk label requires review, approval, strict gate, release gate, or security gate

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for standard risk taxonomy metadata.

rows: risk labels describe posture but do not grant or deny runtime execution
permission checks need runtime identity, scope, and trust semantics beyond static metadata
detecting risk from code or payloads requires scanning and inference outside this vocabulary
human approval routing requires workflow state, receipts, and authority assignment
cost measurement requires provider-specific metering and quota state

artifacts.usage: Minimum exposure classes and upgrade gates for local-only and remote-capable stable surfaces.

rows: consumer_scope, allowed_locations, risk_floor, redaction_posture, freshness_posture, compatibility_posture, non_remote_reason
local agent, repo, diagnostic, debug, or evidence-only consumption
export_format, artifact_scope, export_boundary, redaction_posture, freshness_posture, compatibility_posture, consumer_guidance
generated file or artifact shared only within a declared export boundary
exposure_class, auth_boundary, redaction_floor, freshness_requirement, compatibility_policy, risk_labels, audit_anchor
remote, API, hosted, or adapter consumer may read the surface
command_ref, receipt_ref, event_or_projection_ref, idempotency_posture, policy_decision, approval_gate, rollback_posture, production_mutation_risk
remote consumer may mutate state through a command, adapter, resource, or workflow
adapter_id, capability_id, contract_profile, schema_version_policy, transport_scope, redaction, auth, freshness, compatibility, risk_labels, evidence_anchor
adapter capability claim may be consumed as stable
negotiation session or dynamic provider discovery
source_surface, target_exposure_class, replacement_authority_or_review, risk_labels, redaction, auth, freshness, compatibility, audit_anchor, consumer_guidance
promotion from local-only or local-export to remote-read, remote-write, or adapter-facing use

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred runtime surfaces for local-only versus remote-capable exposure constraints.

rows: exposure metadata describes whether a surface may be read remotely but does not host an API boundary
auth fields describe boundary expectations but do not evaluate identity or permissions
network risk labels describe remote exposure and egress posture but do not broker traffic
local-only to remote-capable promotion requires declared authority or review, not automatic inference
transport execution needs protocol, retry, identity, and operational behavior beyond static metadata

artifacts.usage: Minimum audit metadata for auditable command and adapter-facing surfaces.

rows: actor_id or actor_role, actor_kind, delegated_by when applicable
identifies who or what requested the action
identifies the surface that initiated the action
identifies what object, resource, environment, or endpoint was affected
links audit metadata to the requested command or adapter action
records whether the action was allowed, denied, blocked, or review-required
links audit records to receipts/events and observable consequences
audit log store or event bus runtime
orders action attempts and outcomes across command and adapter traces
joins related command, adapter, receipt, event, and failure records

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred runtime surfaces for command and adapter audit-log requirements.

rows: metadata requirements describe trace shape but do not persist audit records
source and correlation fields identify workflow context but do not execute or schedule workflows
security analytics integration needs vendor-specific transport, identity, and retention behavior
timestamps and correlation identifiers make traces joinable but do not provide telemetry storage or querying
policy decision fields record posture but do not evaluate policy

artifacts.usage: Minimum lifecycle state-machine profile fields for long-running operational objects.

rows: distinguishes authoritative lifecycle state from human-facing status
defines where an operational object may start
defines when normal state transitions stop
from_state, to_state, trigger, actor_boundary, authority_surface, event_or_receipt_ref
defines allowed state movement and trace linkage
precondition, policy_gate, risk_gate, approval_gate, freshness_check, dependency_check
defines conditions that must hold before transition
timeout_at, expires_at, staleness, timeout_action, expiration_action, consumer_guidance
defines how failed or pending attempts may repeat
defines who can cancel and what state results
defines resource cleanup and retained audit posture
links state changes to events or receipts

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred runtime surfaces for lifecycle state-machine profiles.

rows: profile metadata defines allowed states and transitions but does not execute workflows
timeout and expiration fields describe timing posture but do not run timers
retry policy metadata describes repeat behavior but does not perform retries
cleanup semantics describe resource posture but do not delete or mutate resources
state identity describes durable shape but does not provide storage or locking

artifacts.usage: Minimum observability metadata fields for validation and routing decisions.

rows: identifies which validation or routing rule produced the decision
tells consumers whether the decision blocks, warns, advises, or informs
file, json_pointer, section_ref, artifact_ref, line, column
anchors diagnostics to the smallest available source location
explains dependency-driven failures, skips, and degraded routes
explains why a route selected, skipped, degraded, or fell back
explains why a module entered the active working set
explains why a module was omitted from the active working set
points agents or maintainers to the smallest next remediation or review step

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred runtime surfaces for validation and routing observability.

rows: observability fields define explanation vocabulary but do not change text or JSON output formats
selected and skipped module metadata can feed reports but does not build a visual UI
correlation and dependency metadata may be recorded by future tools but this module does not store traces
dependency paths explain current decisions but do not persist or query a dependency graph
routing reason explains current selection posture but does not change ranking behavior

artifacts.usage: Field map for the implementation topology pilot.

rows: id, locator, role, branch, status, notes
repo_id, paths, status, pr_refs, authority_surface, evidence[], acceptance_criteria[]
landed in U-009; evidence landed in U-027; acceptance criteria landed in U-029
total, satisfied, planned, partial, waived, blocked, manual_review
linked_modules, unlinked_modules, checked_paths, missing_paths, evidence_total, checked_evidence_locators, missing_evidence_locators, unchecked_reason
landed in U-010; evidence counters landed in U-027

artifacts.usage: Minimum fields for artifact-level decision provenance boundaries.

rows: agent-consumable, human-review, or internal decision surface
source-evidence, derived-summary, or mixed decision basis
sections or artifacts that authorize the decision
sections or artifacts that prove or audit the decision
section or artifact that owns the derived summary
current, stale, unresolved, or withheld evidence posture
stable, review-required, blocked, or experimental consumption rule
review anchors that justify consuming a derived decision

artifacts.usage: Classify stable-surface changes before version compatibility review.

rows: deprecate first or provide migration guidance
treat as new contract or versioned migration

artifacts.usage: Minimum field guide for adapter-facing capability claims.

rows: stable identifier for the adapter or provider surface
named capability such as route, validate, export, or query
local-only, hosted, or hybrid execution posture
tells consumers whether the claim is remote, local, or mixed
keeps adapter claims tied to an existing contract model
strict, versioned, or experimental compatibility posture
tells consumers how capability changes should be interpreted
tells consumers how stale the capability claim may become
sensitive-data handling posture for capability output
prevents stable adapter claims from bypassing redaction governance
authority or evidence surface that justifies the capability claim
keeps adapter claims reviewable instead of marketing-shaped

artifacts.usage: Metadata-only boundary for capability negotiation re-triage.

rows: capability_id, contract_profile, schema_version_policy, transport_scope, freshness_posture, redaction_posture, limits, evidence_anchor
capability_id, accepted_contract_profile, required_schema_version_policy, transport_scope, freshness_requirement, redaction_floor, blocking
capability_id match, compatible profile, compatible schema policy, compatible transport, freshness meets requirement, redaction meets floor

artifacts.usage: Explicit deferred surfaces for capability negotiation.

rows: requires a protocol surface and trust boundary beyond static metadata
requires credential, scope, and transport security semantics
future security and adapter protocol task
requires executing provider behavior and defining retry or timeout posture
requires ranking, fallback, and conflict policy across multiple providers

artifacts.usage: Deterministic validation posture for stable-surface contract metadata.

rules.condition: manifest.modules[].redaction exists XOR module.meta.redaction exists
rules.action: L2 error: redaction summary must be mirrored

rules.condition: module.meta.redaction.has_sensitive_payloads == true AND missing sensitive_classes or exposure_policy
rules.action: L2 error: sensitive modules must declare classes and exposure policy

rules.condition: manifest.modules[].contract exists XOR module.meta.contract exists
rules.action: L2 error: contract profile summary must be mirrored

rules.condition: module.meta.contract.profile == read-model AND missing module.meta.contract.read_model.freshness
rules.action: L2 error: stable read-model contracts must declare freshness and watermark metadata

rules.condition: stable write-capable command surface claims auditability but lacks command, receipt, event_or_projection, or explicit not-applicable linkage
rules.action: future L3 warning: write-capable command surfaces should expose triad linkage before stable consumption

rules.condition: stable append-only event surface claims auditability but lacks correction, supersession, retraction, or projection guidance semantics
rules.action: future L3 warning: append-only event surfaces should expose correction and supersession posture before stable consumption

rules.condition: stable surface declares partial implementation or known gap but lacks missing_capabilities, blocking_status, owner, expected_remediation, or consumer guidance
rules.action: future L3 warning: partial or known-gap surfaces should expose enough metadata for consumers to decide use, downgrade, review, or block

rules.condition: manifest.modules[].schema_versioning exists XOR module.meta.schema_versioning exists
rules.action: L2 error: schema versioning summary must be mirrored

rules.condition: stable overlapping surfaces declare competing authority without canonical_authority, derived_surface, alias_surface, conflict_policy, or migration_guidance posture
rules.action: future L3 warning: overlapping stable surfaces should expose authority hierarchy before consumers merge claims

rules.condition: stable surface declares dependency on another surface without relationship type, dependency_strength, target_surface, missing_behavior, or fallback/degraded posture for optional dependencies
rules.action: future L3 warning: stable surface dependencies should expose ordering and degraded-use posture before routing or stable consumption

rules.condition: stable surface is deprecated or removed without replacement_ref or no_replacement_reason, migration_guidance, affected_versions, removal_version, and validation_behavior
rules.action: future L3 warning: deprecated or removed stable surfaces should expose migration and validation behavior before consumers move

rules.condition: agent-consumable read, write, adapter, or resource surface lacks declared risk family, risk level, risk source, consumer floor, or human approval posture
rules.action: future L3 warning: agent-consumable surfaces should expose standard risk labels before routing, adapter, or release policy compares them

rules.condition: local-only or local-export surface is referenced by remote-read, remote-write, or adapter-facing consumer without exposure_class, upgrade_gate, redaction, auth, freshness, compatibility, and risk posture
rules.action: future L3 warning: local-only surfaces should not be reused by remote consumers without explicit exposure upgrade gates

rules.condition: auditable command, remote-write, or adapter-facing surface lacks actor, source, target, command_ref, idempotency_key, policy_decision, receipt_ref, timestamp, or correlation identifiers
rules.action: future L3 warning: commands and adapters should expose audit metadata before consumers rely on action reconstruction

rules.condition: long-running operational object lacks lifecycle state, display status distinction, initial state, terminal states, transitions, guards, timeout/expiration, retry, cancellation, cleanup, or event/receipt links
rules.action: future L3 warning: long-running operational objects should expose lifecycle state-machine profile metadata before consumers rely on state transitions

rules.condition: validation or routing decision lacks rule id, severity, source location, dependency path, routing reason, selected module, skipped module, or suggested next action metadata
rules.action: future L3 warning: validation and routing decisions should expose machine-readable observability metadata before agents rely on explanation or debug output

rules.condition: manifest.modules[].implementation exists XOR module.meta.implementation exists
rules.action: L2 error: implementation linkage summary must be mirrored

rules.condition: duplicate ids exist in manifest.project_topology.implementation_repos[]
rules.action: L2 error: implementation repository ids must be unique

rules.condition: module.meta.implementation exists AND resolve(module.meta.implementation.repo_id, manifest.project_topology.implementation_repos) == false
rules.action: L2 error: implementation repo linkage must resolve to manifest.project_topology.implementation_repos[]

rules.condition: module.meta.implementation.status == current AND module.meta.implementation.evidence[] is empty
rules.action: L2 error: current implementation linkage must declare evidence anchors

rules.condition: validate --reality can resolve implementation repo root AND path-like evidence locator is missing
rules.action: L2 error: path-like implementation evidence locators must exist

rules.condition: artifact.decision_provenance exists AND any source_refs target is unresolved
rules.action: L2 error: decision provenance source refs must resolve to sections or artifacts

rules.condition: artifact.decision_provenance exists AND any evidence_refs target is unresolved
rules.action: L2 error: decision provenance evidence refs must resolve to sections or artifacts

rules.condition: artifact.decision_provenance.summary_ref exists AND summary_ref target is unresolved
rules.action: L2 error: decision provenance summary_ref must resolve to a section or artifact

rules.condition: artifact.decision_provenance.basis is derived-summary or mixed AND summary_ref is missing
rules.action: L2 error: derived decision provenance must identify the summary owner

rules.condition: artifact.decision_provenance.consumer_surface == agent-consumable AND consumption_gate == stable AND evidence_status IN stale, unresolved, withheld
rules.action: L2 error: stable agent-consumable decisions require current evidence posture

changelog.delta: Initial stable-surface contract module for redaction, minimal profiles, topology pilot design, and version compatibility.
Landed root topology schema semantics and added the minimum adapter-facing capability contract boundary.
Landed module implementation linkage and topology-aware reality summary semantics for the design-to-reality pilot.
Clarified that implementation repository identifiers are unique topology identities.
Added declarative implementation evidence anchors, manifest evidence summaries, and reality locator checks for path-like evidence.
Added implementation acceptance criteria as contract-to-evidence linkage with manifest acceptance summaries.
Added artifact-level decision provenance boundaries for agent-consumable decisions and derived summaries.
Added read-model freshness and watermark metadata requirements for stable read-model contracts.
Re-triaged capability negotiation into metadata-only provider, consumer, compatibility matching, and evidence-link boundaries.
Added write-capable command receipt event triad boundaries for command audit linkage.
Added append-only event correction and supersession boundaries for stable event surfaces.
Added partial implementation and known-gap metadata boundaries for stable consumption posture.
Added ownership and authority hierarchy boundaries for overlapping stable surfaces.
Added dependency ordering boundaries for stable surfaces that require, block, derive from, emit, consume, or optionally depend on other surfaces.
Added deprecation and migration metadata boundaries for deprecated, replaced, and removed stable surfaces.
Added standard risk taxonomy boundaries for agent-consumable read, write, adapter, and resource surfaces.
Added local-only versus remote-capable exposure constraints for local exports, remote reads, remote writes, and adapter-facing surfaces.
Added audit-log requirements for command and adapter action traces, including actor, source, target, idempotency, policy, receipt, timestamp, and correlation metadata.
Added lifecycle state-machine profile boundaries for long-running operational objects.
Added validation and routing observability metadata boundaries for rule, severity, source, dependency, route selection, skip, and next-action explanation.

read_model.absence_semantics: Missing sections mean the profile is not defined in this module.
read_model.failure_semantics: Validation fails when required profile metadata is absent.
read_model.partial_semantics: Partial snapshots must not be consumed as stable contract authority.

schema_versioning.compatibility_window: minor-version line until deprecation guidance says otherwise
